2606.06360.title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
2606.06360.paper_summary: Modelling individual decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing effective public health interventions. Prior work has shown that large language models can simulate realistic human behaviour by generating agent decisions based on demographic prompts and situational context. We build on this foundation with a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework that integrates LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population of agents. Location is treated as a central feature: agents are assigned to spatial units within cities, capturing the spatial distributions of different demographic groups using real-world census data and enabling geographically diverse behavioural modelling. We implement and compare three decision scenarios, independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing, and simulate self-reporting outcomes in San Francisco and Atlanta. Results reveal that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. Our framework generates synthetic data that captures both social and geographic heterogeneity, supporting spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis.
2606.06360.paper_summary_zh: 建模傳染病爆發期間的個體決策對於理解行為動態和提供有效的公共衛生干預至關重要。先前的研究顯示，大型語言模型可以通過根據人口統計提示和情境背景生成代理決策來模擬現實的人類行為。我們在這一基礎上構建了一個基於空間的代理模型模擬框架，將LLM生成的有關自我報告流感樣疾病的決策整合到基於人口普查的合成代理人口中。位置被視為一個核心特徵：代理被分配到城市中的空間單位，捕捉不同人口群體的空間分布，使用現實世界的人口普查數據，並實現地理多樣的行為建模。我們實施並比較三種決策情境：獨立推理、家庭影響和信息框架，並模擬在舊金山和亞特蘭大的自我報告結果。結果顯示，收入和教育是報告率變化的主要驅動因素，地理、LLM模型選擇和信息框架的影響較小但一致。我們的框架生成的合成數據捕捉了社會和地理異質性，支持空間流行病學建模和偏見意識的行為分析。
2606.06360.author: Yonchanok Khaokaew et.al.
2606.06360.authors: Yonchanok Khaokaew, Ruochen Kong, Andreas Zufle, Hao Xue, Taylor Anderson, Chandini Raina MacIntyre, Matthew Scotch, Flora D. Salim, David J Heslop
